text.title: go-goroutines Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for go-goroutines. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating. Use channels as the primary coordination mechanism. Avoid raw shared mutable state protected only by locks.
rule.rationale: Channel-based communication is easier to reason about and prevents whole categories of race conditions and deadlock scenarios.

rule.statement: Use channels for goroutine coordination. Goroutines communicate via channels; the type system enforces correct usage (send-only, receive-only).
rule.rationale: Explicit channels in function signatures make dependencies clear and the compiler catches misuse.

rule.statement: Keep critical sections small. When you must use mutex, hold the lock for the minimum time necessary. Do not call blocking functions inside a lock.
rule.rationale: Long critical sections reduce parallelism and increase contention. Blocking inside a lock can deadlock other goroutines waiting for the lock.

rule.statement: Avoid goroutine leaks. Always provide an exit condition for long-lived goroutines. Include context.Done() checks in every loop that may block on I/O or channels.
rule.rationale: Goroutine leaks exhaust memory and system resources. They are hard to detect in production. Explicit exit conditions prevent them.

rule.statement: Use context for cancellation. Propagate context through function signatures so cancellation and deadlines can cascade down call stacks.
rule.rationale: Context is the standard Go mechanism for managing request lifecycle, timeouts, and cancellation across function boundaries.

rule.statement: Worker pool MUST use sync.WaitGroup to track completion. Close the results channel only after all workers have exited. Include context.Done() cases in every select to allow cancellation.
rule.rationale: WaitGroup ensures we don't close results prematurely. Context allows graceful shutdown. Together they prevent goroutine leaks.

rule.statement: Inventory all goroutines spawned in a package. For each: lifetime, exit condition, input owner, output owner, max wait on send, panic policy. Reject any row missing exit condition or panic policy.
rule.rationale: This audit prevents leaks and makes goroutine lifecycle explicit.

rule.statement: Every test that spawns goroutines MUST use defer goleak.VerifyNone(t) to assert no goroutines leak. Run tests with go test -race to detect data races.
rule.rationale: Race detector finds timing-dependent bugs at runtime. Goleak ensures test cleanup does not leak goroutines.

rule.statement: Run with -count=10 on any test that checks concurrency. This disables test caching and reruns the test multiple times, surfacing flaky race conditions.
rule.rationale: Timing-dependent bugs may only manifest under certain scheduling conditions. Multiple runs increase the chance of detecting them.

rule.statement: Include test cases for cancellation, panic in jobs, and slow consumers. Verify the pool shuts down cleanly and no goroutines leak under all scenarios.
rule.rationale: These edge cases are where most production bugs hide.
